Its internal search algorithm is fundamentally based on Lazy Clause Generation (LCG), which incrementally encodes integer variables and constraints into Boolean clauses [2][4]. \n\nKey components include a presolve phase that simplifies the model and a portfolio of workers that run diverse search strategies in parallel [5][2]. These workers use branching heuristics, such as pseudo-costs or LP-based reduced cost fixing, to explore the search tree, while sharing information like bounds and discovered solutions [5][2][3]. The solver performs exact propagation using 64-bit integer arithmetic to avoid floating-point inaccuracies, and optional simplex-based linear relaxation can tighten variable bounds and guide the search [1][6][3].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003e\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.mixedinteger.org/EUROMIP/2025/slides/frederic-didier.pdf\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eOR-tools\n[...]\n-SAT solver.\n[...]\nA Breakthrough: CP vs CP-SAT\n[...]\nExploiting SAT solvers recent progress: Conflict Directed Clause Learning (CDLC)\n[...]\nInspired by Chuffed and by Peter Stuckey's “Search is dead, long live proof”\n[...]\n\u0026 MaxSAT:\n[...]\n● Core based search\n● Model reductions\n● Clause Learning\n[...]\n● Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)\n● Violation based Local Search (from MIP feasibility jump)\n[...]\nCP-SAT is an EXACT solver\n[...]\nEven though LP solver is inexact, we just use its output as a “hint”.\n[...]\n● We use only int64/int128 arithmetic. \n● No epsilon! But have to deal with integer overflow.\n[...]\n● vs MIP: simplify the code \u0026 complexity a lot.\n[...]\nSame for the cuts, we compute everything with integers.\n[...]\nCompared to a MIP solver: \n● Everything is integer (int64) and bounded. \n● integer-overflow precondition: min/max constraint activity fit on int64.\n[...]\n● The constraints do not need to describe the full problem.\n[...]\nIngredient 2 : Linear combination of constraints\n[...]\nPropagating dual-infeasible LP\n[...]\n● Take dual ray (aka infeasibility proof) as constraint multipliers\n[...]\n● New constraint should give rise to a conflict (i.e. min_activity \u003e rhs).\n[...]\nNote: Even if not conflicting, it will likely have small\n[...]\ncan thus propagates bounds\n[...]\nating dual-feasible LP\n[...]\nnegated dual LP values (scaled by s) as constraint multipliers\n[...]\n_rhs [\n[...]\nobjective_var \u003c= new\n[...]\nNormal CP-SAT propagation of this new constraint\n[...]\nGeneric LP Cuts\n[...]\nGeneral MIP cut framework\n[...]\nAlmost all cuts (gomory, MIR, zero half, cover, flow, …) follow this:\n[...]\n1. Aggregate many constraints from the integer lp into one (terms \u003c= rhs) \n2. From such single constraint, rewrite it slightly with complementation, implied \nbounds, and using positive variables. \n3. Apply a super-additive function f() to get the cut.\n[...]\n4. Rewrite everything in term of the original variables (i.e. undo step\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eHow does it work? - The CP-SAT Primer - Using and Understanding Google OR-Tools' CP-SAT Solver\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://d-krupke.github.io/cpsat-primer/07_under_the_hood.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eCP-SAT is a versatile portfolio solver, centered around a Lazy Clause Generation (LCG) based Constraint Programming\n[...]\nCP-SAT employs Branch and Cut techniques, including linear relaxations and cutting planes, as part of its toolkit. Models that can be efficiently addressed by a Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) solver are typically a good match for CP-SAT as well. Nevertheless, CP-SAT’s central focus is the implementation of Lazy Clause Generation, harnessing SAT-solvers rather than relying primarily on linear relaxations. As a result, CP-SAT may exhibit somewhat reduced performance when confronted with MIP problems compared to dedicated MIP solvers. However, it gains a distinct advantage when dealing with problems laden with intricate logical constraints.\n[...]\nSolution Search:\n[...]\nSeveral full subsol\n[...]\nare run across\n[...]\nThese strategies may include:\n[...]\nFirst Solution Search and Local Search:\n[...]\nAs already mentioned before, CP-SAT also utilizes the (dual) simplex algorithm and linear relaxations. The linear relaxation is implemented as a propagator and potentially executed at every node in the search tree but only at lowest priority. A significant difference to the application of linear relaxations in branch and bound algorithms is that only some pivot iterations are performed (to make it faster). However, as there are likely much deeper search trees and the warm-starts are utilized, the optimal linear relaxation may still be computed, just deeper down the tree (note that for SAT-solving, the search tree is usually traversed DFS). At root level, even cutting planes such as Gomory-Cuts are applied to improve the linear relaxation.\n[...]\nThe linear relaxation is used for detecting infe\n[...]\ns can actually be more powerful than simple SAT, at least in theory), finding better bounds for\n[...]\nobjective and variables, and also for making branching decisions (using the linear relaxation’s objective and the reduced costs).\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eOR-Tools\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://egon.cheme.cmu.edu/ewo/docs/CP-SAT%20and%20OR-Tools.pdf\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eCP-SAT-LP\n[...]\nConstraint Programming solver\n[...]\nInteger Linear Programming solver\n[...]\na \nCDCL SAT solver.\n[...]\nour CPAIOR\n[...]\nThe CP-SAT-LP solver architecture\n[...]\nA complete reimplementation of a Constraint \nProgramming Solver on top of a SAT engine. \nA rich modeling layer that offers linear arithmetic \nand more complex discrete optimization \nconstraints (including scheduling and routing \nconstraints). \nComplemented by a Simplex that solves a \n(dynamic) linear relaxation of the problem. \nEnriched by MIP techniques (presolve, cuts, \nbranching heuristics) extended to cover the \nConstraint Programming layer. \n\nSAT\n[...]\nPortfolio of Search heuristics and Large Neighborhood Search\n[...]\nBounds propagation (with SAT explanations): \n• LP infeasibility \n• Objective lower bound \n• Variables lower/upper bound (reduced cost fixing) \nHeuristics: \n• Branching: LP optimal value \u0026 reduced costs can be used. \n• Large neighborhood search (i.e. RINS). \n\nOur propagation is EXACT !\n[...]\npart of the CP propagation algorithm. Bounds are\n[...]\n● CP-SAT-LP is a very good CP solver \n● In CP-SAT-LP, the linear relaxation is on by default. \n● Clause Learning is critical as it offsets the cost of running the simplex. \n● It implements the Scheduling on MIP literature from the 80s, 90s and \nsubsequent improvements.\n[...]\n● It could incorporate different relaxation/specialized (expensive) propagation\n[...]\nalgorithms, provided they can be explained, or\n[...]\nthey can be useful at\n[...]\nWhat's next? \n● Reduce encoding size \n● Incorporate cost management techniques from CSP solvers \n● MIP improvements (cuts, presolve) \n● Improved scheduling (branching heuristics, LNS, propagation) \n● Incremental violation local search on non linear constraints \n● Non violation based Local Search? \n● Progress on Routing (branch\n[...]\nheuristics, LNS, propagation)\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003e🎓 How the CP-SAT solver works | Just another developer\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://xiang.es/posts/explaining-cp-sat/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eOverview of the CP-SAT solver from Google OR-Tools\n[...]\n- Awesome OR-Tools: https://github.com/or-tools/awesome_or-tools#algorithms\n- Laurent Perron and Frédéric Didier, CPAIOR 2020: https://youtu.be/lmy1ddn4cyw\n- Peter J. Stuckey, Search is Dead: https://people.eng.unimelb.edu.au/pstuckey/PPDP2013.pdf\n- Dominik Krupke, CP-SAT primer: https://github.com/d-krupke/cpsat-primer\n[...]\n## Presolve Loop\n[...]\nFirst stage: We will process all active constraints until a fix point is reached. During this stage:\n[...]\n1. Presolve: Domain reduction, constraint simplification/rewrite\n2. Constraint expansion/decomposition: Similar to Minizinc -\u003e Flatzinc (element constraint, table, automaton, inverse, product, modulo, reservoir)\n3. Detect variable equivalence and affine relations.\n4. Substitute by canonical representation.\n5. Probing: Fix variables and see what is propagated.\n[...]\nThis produces 2 new models, the inner model that will be solved and a channeling model used to populate the solution of the initial model. - source\n[...]\nThe CP-SAT solver uses a lazy clause generation solver on top of an SAT solver. The best description is a presentation from Peter Stuckey called Search is Dead- Laurent Perron\n[...]\nIn Lazy clause generation (LCG), integer variables are encoded as booleans, ortools creates 2 booleans for each variable and value:\n[...]\n- var == value\n- var \u003c= value\n[...]\n- (var == value) \u003c=\u003e (var \u003e= value) and (var \u003c= value)\n- (var \u003c= value) =\u003e (var \u003c= value+1)\n[...]\nPropagation is clause generation:\n[...]\n- e.g. [x \u003c= 2] and x \u003e= y means that [y \u003c= 2]\n- clause [x \u003c= 2] =\u003e [y \u003c= 2]\n[...]\n## Default search (single thread)\n[...]\nVSIDS on the Boolean problem, when it reaches a fixed point, it asks the heuristic to select an integer variable, a value and a braching direction.\n[...]\n## Multithreading\n[...]\nThe solver uses the first X threads to generic methods, and use all the remaining ones on LNS (Large Neighborhood Search). - Laurent Perron\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eCP-SAT Core Engine | google/or-tools | DeepWiki\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://deepwiki.com/google/or-tools/4.1.1-cp-sat-core-engine\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eThis page documents the core execution engine of the CP-SAT solver, covering how a &#96;CpModelProto&#96; is transformed, loaded, and solved. This includes the presolve phase, model loading into internal data structures, search orchestration, LP integration for bound tightening, and parallel solving architecture.\n[...]\nThe CP-SAT core engine processes a constraint programming model through several phases, from initial presolve transformations to parallel search with LP-based bound tightening.\n[...]\nAfter presolve, the &#96;CpModelProto&#96; is loaded into internal solver data structures. This creates Boolean and integer variables, instantiates propagators, and establishes the mapping between proto indices and internal solver variables.\n[...]\nThe main solving loop is orchestrated by the &#96;SolveCpModel()&#96; function in &#96;cp\\_model\\_solver.cc&#96;. This function sets up the search, manages parallel workers, and handles solution reporting.\n[...]\nThe core search loop alternates between making decisions, propagating constraints, and handling conflicts.\n[...]\n## Search Strategies\n[...]\nCP-SAT supports both user-defined search strategies and automatic heuristics. The search strategy determines which variable to branch on and what value to try first.\n[...]\nUsers can specify search strategies in the &#96;CpModelProto&#96; via the &#96;search\\_strategy&#96; field. These are processed by &#96;ConstructUserSearchStrategy()&#96;.\n[...]\n### Automatic Heuristics\n[...]\nWhen no user strategy is specified, CP-SAT uses automatic heuristics based on variable activities, pseudo-costs, and impact analysis.\n[...]\nCP-SAT uses linear programming relaxations to tighten variable bounds and guide search. The &#96;LinearProgrammingConstraint&#96; class manages this integration.\n[...]\n### LP Propagation Flow\n[...]\nCP-SAT uses a portfolio approach to parallel solving, running multiple search strategies simultaneously and sharing solutions and bounds between workers.\n[...]\n### Solution and Bound\n[...]\n### Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",
      "title": "Web search results"
    },

[Understanding CP-SAT: Constraint Programming Meets SAT Solving | by Jonas Poelmans | Medium](https://medium.com/@jonaspoelmans120/understanding-cp-sat-constraint-programming-meets-sat-solving-b137dddb943c)\n2. [Constraint Programming (CP)](https://www.hexaly.com/modeling-paradigms/constraint-programming-cp)\n4. [Towards Modern and Modular SAT for LCG](https://drops.dagstuhl.de/storage/00lipics/lipics-vol340-cp2025/html/LIPIcs.CP.2025.42/LIPIcs.CP.2025.42.html)\n5. [A Customized SAT-based Solver for Graph Coloring](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.04821v2)\n\nSynthesis:\nCP-SAT is a high-performance solver for discrete optimization that combines constraint programming with SAT-based techniques [1][2]. It uses lazy clause generation to translate integer variable constraints into a clausal format, which allows it to leverage conflict-driven clause learning (CDCL) and non-chronological backtracking [1][2]. This hybrid architecture enables the solver to prune search spaces through logical inference while efficiently learning from dead ends [1][3]. Recent advancements in the field include the IPASIR-UP interface, which allows developers to integrate custom propagators directly into modern SAT engines [4][5].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eUnderstanding CP-SAT: Constraint Programming Meets SAT Solving | by Jonas Poelmans | Medium\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://medium.com/@jonaspoelmans120/understanding-cp-sat-constraint-programming-meets-sat-solving-b137dddb943c\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eConstraint programming represents a fundamentally different approach to solving discrete optimization problems. Rather than approximating discrete decisions through continuous relaxations, constraint programming works directly with discrete domains and uses logical inference to eliminate impossible values. Modern constraint programming solvers like Google’s CP-SAT combine classical constraint propagation with sophisticated SAT solving techniques, creating hybrid solvers that excel at problems with complex logical constraints and tight coupling between decisions.\n[...]\nPropagation: The process of using constraints to automatically eliminate values from domains through logical inference. When one variable is assigned a value, propagation applies all constraints involving that variable to immediately remove values from other variables’ domains that would violate those constraints.\n[...]\nPropagation is the heart of constraint programming. When a variable is assigned a value or when a domain changes, propagation applies all relevant constraints to eliminate values from other variables’ domains that can no longer be part of a feasible solution.\n[...]\nModern CP-SAT solvers achieve exceptional performance through integration with Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) solving techniques. This combination creates a hybrid approach more powerful than either technique alone.\n[...]\nThe key technique that makes SAT solving efficient is conflict-driven clause learning (CDCL). When the solver hits a dead end (a conflict where no valid assignment exists), it analyzes why the conflict occurred and learns a new clause preventing similar conflicts.\n[...]\nSAT solvers use non-chronological backtracking. The conflict analysis determines which earlier decision actually caused the conflict. If decision 15 is the culprit, the solver jumps directly back to decision 15, even though it made 35 subsequent decisions. All the intermediate work that was valid is kept.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eHow does it work? - The CP-SAT Primer - Using and Understanding Google OR-Tools' CP-SAT Solver\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://d-krupke.github.io/cpsat-primer/07_under_the_hood.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eCP-SAT is a versatile portfolio solver, centered around a Lazy Clause Generation (LCG) based Constraint Programming Solver, although it encompasses a broader spectrum of technologies.\n[...]\nCP-SAT employs Branch and Cut techniques, including linear relaxations and cutting planes, as part of its toolkit. Models that can be efficiently addressed by a Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) solver are typically a good match for CP-SAT as well. Nevertheless, CP-SAT’s central focus is the implementation of Lazy Clause Generation, harnessing SAT-solvers rather than relying primarily on linear relaxations. As a result, CP-SAT may exhibit somewhat reduced performance when confronted with MIP problems compared to dedicated MIP solvers. However, it gains a distinct advantage when dealing with problems laden with intricate logical constraints.\n[...]\nLearn the additional concepts of LCG Constraint Programming: Propagation, Lazy Clause Generation, …\n[...]\n- Higher-level constraints are expanded into lower-level constraints, CP-SAT actually can propagate efficiently, but which are less comfortable for you to write. See FlatZinc and Flattening for a similar process.\n[...]\nAs already mentioned before, CP-SAT also utilizes the (dual) simplex algorithm and linear relaxations. The linear relaxation is implemented as a propagator and potentially executed at every node in the search tree but only at lowest priority. A significant difference to the application of linear relaxations in branch and bound algorithms is that only some pivot iterations are performed (to make it faster). However, as there are likely much deeper search trees and the warm-starts are utilized, the optimal linear relaxation may still be computed, just deeper down the tree (note that for SAT-solving, the search tree is usually traversed DFS). At root level, even cutting planes such as Gomory-Cuts are applied to improve the linear relaxation.\n[...]\nis used for\n[...]\nsimple SAT, at\n[...]\nand the reduced\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eConstraint Programming (CP)\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.hexaly.com/modeling-paradigms/constraint-programming-cp\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eConstraint Programming (CP) is a powerful paradigm for modeling and solving combinatorial decision problems. Unlike mathematical programming approaches such as Linear Programming (LP), Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP), or Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP), constraint programming focuses on expressing logical relationships and combinatorial structure directly, rather than optimizing a numerical objective under algebraic constraints.\n[...]\nThe core mechanism behind Constraint Programming (CP) is constraint propagation. When a variable’s domain is reduced, constraints infer and remove inconsistent values from other variables’ domains. This pruning reduces the search space dramatically before and during search.\n[...]\nOnce propagation alone cannot determine a solution, Constraint Programming (CP) solvers employ backtracking search, systematically exploring variable assignments. Modern solvers enhance this with:\n[...]\n- Domain splitting and branching heuristics,\n- Conflict-directed backtracking,\n- Nogood learning and techniques inspired by SAT solving, such as Conflict-Driven Clause Learning (CDCL).\n[...]\nThese techniques differ markedly from Branch-and-Bound or Cutting-Plane methods used in MILP, making Constraint Programming (CP) complementary rather than competing in many applications. Indeed, Constraint Programming (CP) is commonly applied to combinatorial problems characterized by discrete variables and complex logical constraints. By relying on constraint propagation and backtracking search rather than mathematical relaxations, it offers an alternative modeling and solution paradigm that complements linear, nonlinear, and mixed-integer optimization techniques.\n[...]\n- It does not rely on relaxations, duality, or convexity.\n- It naturally models discrete domains and logical constraints.\n- It is closely related to SAT and SMT solving, sharing techniques such as clause learning and backtracking.\n[...]\n- Hybrid CP/MILP\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eTowards Modern and Modular SAT for LCG\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://drops.dagstuhl.de/storage/00lipics/lipics-vol340-cp2025/html/LIPIcs.CP.2025.42/LIPIcs.CP.2025.42.html\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eLazy Clause Generation [31] is an architecture for building Constraint Programming solvers by making use of an underlying SAT engine [27]. LCG solvers such as Chuffed [10] and CP-SAT [32] define the state-of-the-art for constraint programming and have consistently scored the most points in the Free search category in the MiniZinc challenge ever since 2010 when one was first entered.\n[...]\nHowever, the SAT infrastructure used in LCG solvers is mainly based on SAT technology from at least a decade ago. Meanwhile, SAT solvers have made significant advances, including inprocessing [24], chronological backtracking [29], as well as improvement to search strategies by including local search components [9].\n[...]\nASIR-UP\n[...]\nIt is well known that the search strategy determined by the branching heuristic used can have an enormous impact on solving performance. Using the IPASIR-UP’s decide callback, the LCG backend can influence the search strategy. Most prominently, this method allows the backend to choose a literal to branch on. It can also be used to request the solver to restart, returning to a state where no decisions had been made. If no decision is made by the LCG backend, then the search strategy is left up to the SAT solver. Generally, it will make its decisions based on the variable state independent decaying sum (VSIDS) heuristic [28], and will use its own policy for restarts unless disabled.\n[...]\nIn this section, we revisit a key decision in the construction LCG solvers, the way in which propagation is explained to the SAT engine. Chuffed uses both forward and backward explanation, and Huub implements the same strategy by default. However, with modern SAT solvers becoming increasingly efficient at handling clauses, pushing more clauses into the SAT engine can potentially enhance clause simplification and improve variable selection during search. To leverage these advantages, we implement a hybrid approach that combines clausal and backward explanation.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eA Customized SAT-based Solver for Graph Coloring\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://arxiv.org/html/2504.04821v2\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eWe introduce ZykovColor, a novel SAT-based algorithm to solve the graph coloring problem working on top of an encoding that mimics the Zykov tree. Our method is based on an approach of Hébrard and Katsirelos (2020) that employs a propagator to enforce transitivity constraints, incorporate lower bounds for search tree pruning, and enable inferred propagations.\n[...]\nWe leverage the recently introduced IPAS\n[...]\nCaDiCaL to implement these\n[...]\nwith a SAT solver.\n[...]\nSAT solver.\n[...]\ndecision strategy with vertex domination hints and using incremental bottom-up search that\n[...]\ncalls. Additionally, we integrate a more\n[...]\nclique computation and an algorithm for computing the fractional chromatic number to improve\n[...]\nVarious exact algorithms have been proposed to address the graph coloring problem. Examples are the branch-and-bound variant DSATUR [6, 32, 13], branch-and-price [30, 27, 18, 20] (based on the Zykov recursion [39]), integer programming [31, 9, 22], SAT solvers [35, 16, 21, 11], constraint programming solvers [19], or binary decision diagrams [36].\n[...]\nOur approach is also based on the Zykov recursion [39] that was already used in conjunction with SAT solvers [34, 16] and constrained programming [19], where the authors introduce a hybrid approach that combines constraint programming with SAT-solving techniques such as clause learning.\n[...]\nIn this work, we adapt the propagator for the graph coloring problem presented in [19]. Originally proposed in conjunction with a hybrid CP/SAT approach, we adopt it to be used with the state-of-the-art SAT solver CaDiCaL [4], heavily using the recently introduced IPASIR-UP interface [12] (Sections˜3.1, 3.2 and 3.3). We use the features of the interface to present ZykovColor, a customized SAT-based solver for the graph coloring problem.\n[...]\nImplementing custom constraint propagations utilizing the IPASIR-UP interface with lazy (deferred) addition of reason clauses (Section˜3.1),\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

---

## 完成情况

**归档文件**：`docback backup/DOC-2026-001-JMix-ConfigEngine-Constraint-Solving-Design.md`

### 文档结构

文档分为 **7 大章节**，覆盖了你要求的全部核心内容：

| 章节 | 内容 | 页数估算 |
|---|---|---|
| **第1章 业务问题** | 液压系统配置场景、数控机床多规则冲突场景，详细说明"部分满足"和"冲突定位"两大核心痛点 | ~2页 |
| **第2章 约束求解方案** | 从业务规则到数学模型的转换、松弛变量策略、冲突诊断策略 | ~1.5页 |
| **第3章 约束生成原理** | 四层数据模型、三类变量系统（Para/Part/PartCategory）、9种规则Schema的CP-SAT映射、线性表达式构建、优先级目标函数、完整实例演示（液压站配置） | ~4页 |
| **第4章 OR-Tools搜索算法** | CP-SAT整体架构图、Presolve五步流程、整数变量布尔编码（双序列编码）、约束传播原理、LCG懒子句生成机制、CDCL冲突分析与非时序回跳、LP松弛+切平面、Portfolio并行搜索、LNS大邻域搜索 | ~5页 |
| **第5章 项目核心实现** | 五层对称架构图、bmodel继承体系表、algmodel松弛变量附加代码分析、执行器完整流程图、冲突诊断两阶段松弛流程 | ~2页 |
| **第6章 术语表** | 18个核心术语的中英文对照与解释 | ~0.5页 |
| **第7章 参考资料** | 8条引用（RFC文档+代码路径+学术论文+OR-Tools官方文档） | ~0.5页 |

## 修改摘要

### 1. 场景替换为电信产品
- **场景一**：从液压泵 → 企业级路由器（转发性能 + 风扇数 + 光模块）
- **场景二**：从数控机床 → 数据中心交换机（交换引擎 + 端口模块 + 电源 + 散热 + 管理平面 + 安全模块）
- **3.5 实例**：从液压站 → 企业路由器配置（转发性能 + 风扇数 + 光模块）

### 2. 去除 `_hidden` 相关内容
- 变量表中删除了 `pt_hidden_{code}` 和 `para_hidden` 两行
- 3.2 变量系统章节删除了 `hidden` 相关段落
- 3.3 约束构建中删除了"默认可见性约束"小节
- 5.3 变量初始化中删除了 `hidden` 相关描述
- 表6-3 变量表中删除了 `pt_hidden`、`para_hidden` 行

### 3. 新增第 3 章 —— 约束求解核心原理通俗图解
这是本次修改中**新增内容最多**的一章，用"三个杯子放糖"的日常例子贯穿讲解：

| 小节 | 内容 |
|---|---|
| 3.1 什么是约束 | 用杯子放糖解释变量、约束、求解 |
| 3.2 从5条规则开始 | 逐步添加规则，验证有解 |
| 3.3 松弛变量 | 给每条规则装开关，附完整代码/伪代码 |
| 3.4 松弛之后 | 求解器看到什么，对比放松不同规则的不同结果 |
| 3.5 冲突诊断 | 从 r_i=1 读出哪些规则被绕过，生成诊断报告 |
| 3.6 小结 | 五步表格总结（建模→求解→加松弛→重新求解→诊断） |

### 4. 目录结构调整
- 原第3章"约束生成原理"顺延为第4章
- 原第4章"OR-Tools搜索算法"顺延为第5章
- 原第5章"项目核心实现"顺延为第6章
- 术语表顺延为第7章，参考资料顺延为第8章
